column2: Back

Mel = 1127 · ln(1 + f/700)
Inverse: f = 700 · (e^(Mel/1127) − 1)

Source: exam2024:Q4, solution_ex1:4b

WER = (S + D + I) / N
S=substitutions, D=deletions, I=insertions, N=words in reference.
Lower is better.

Source: L04:wer, solution_ex3:1i

column1: Mel filter banks: triangular filters — what are start, peak, end?
column2:
For N filters, use N+2 equally spaced Mel points. Filter i peaks at point i+1, spans from point i to point i+2.

Source: exam2024:Q4, solution_ex1:4b

column1:
Exam 2024 Mel filters: 200–8000 Hz, 3 filters, 5 Mel points (i=0..4).
Compute Mel endpoints and first Hz point.
column2:
M_low = 1127·ln(1+200/700) ≈ 301 Mel
M_high = 1127·ln(1+8000/700) ≈ 2840 Mel
Δ = (2840−301)/4 ≈ 635
M₀ ≈ 301 → f₀ ≈ 200 Hz (check: matches lower bound)

Source: exam2024:Q4

column1: What is a phone in speech recognition?
column2:
A speech sound represented by a symbol (ARPAbet/IPA). A string of phones = pronunciation of a word.

Source: L04:phonetics

column1: Relationship between F0, pitch, and the Mel scale?
column2:
F0 = lowest frequency of periodic waveform. Pitch = perceptual correlate of F0 (not linear above ~1 kHz). Mel scale models perceived pitch spacing.

Source: L04:pitch

column1:
Exercise 1 speech: waveform period T=0.2 s, max amplitude A=2.
Compute frequency f and identify amplitude.
column2:
f = 1/T = 1/0.2 = 5 Hz
Amplitude A = 2 (max value on y-axis).

Source: solution_ex1:4a

Window speech → DFT/FFT per frame → power spectrum → Mel filter bank → log → feature vector (e.g. 80-dim).

Source: L04:feature_extraction

Window size: 20–40 ms. Stride: ~50% overlap. Hamming window reduces spectral leakage at frame edges.

Source: L04:windowing

w(n) = 0.54 − 0.46·cos(2πn/(N−1))
Tapers frame edges → minimizes discontinuities and spectral leakage.

Source: L04:hamming

1. Convert f_low, f_high to Mel
2. Space N+2 points linearly in Mel
3. Convert back to Hz
4. Round to FFT bins
5. Create overlapping triangular filters (peak at center point)

Outputs per frame incl. blank token. Collapse consecutive duplicates → many alignments map to same output. Training uses dynamic programming over alignments.

Source: L04:CTC

1. Encoder (acoustic model)
2. Predictor (language model, conditions on output so far)
3. Joiner (feedforward network combining encoder + predictor)

Source: L04:RNN-T

Online decoding: increment output index u on non-blank token, increment acoustic index t on blank — processes audio incrementally.

Source: L04:RNN-T, exam2024:Q1b

Time warping (distort time axis)
Frequency masking (mask mel bands)
Time masking (mask time frames)

Source: L04:augmentation

Sampling: discrete time points.
Quantization: map continuous amplitudes to finite discrete values (can be positive or negative).

Source: solution_ex1:1g, L04:adc

column1: Why subsample acoustic features in encoder-decoder ASR?
column2:
Input acoustic sequence is much longer than output token sequence — subsampling (low frame rate, CNN pooling, pyramidal RNN) compresses time dimension.

Source: L04:subsampling

Substitution: wrong word in place of REF word.
Insertion: extra word in SYS not in REF.
Deletion: REF word missing from SYS.

Source: L04:WER, solution_ex3:1i

column1:

Define filter 1 and filter 2 (start–peak–end).
column2:
Filter 1: 200 – 887 – 2098 Hz
Filter 2: 887 – 2098 – 4234 Hz
Filter 3: 2098 – 4234 – 8000 Hz

Source: exam2024:Q4

column1: Prosody in speech — what does it include?
column2:
Pitch (F0), duration, loudness, rhythm, intonation, prominence — NOT just spectral/formant features.

Source: L04:prosody, exam2025:Q1d

Encoder-decoder Transformer trained on large-scale weakly supervised speech data (multilingual, multitask).
